description: Fix 7 remaining security vulnerabilities identified by comprehensive audit. Covers WebSocket path validation, sender verification, regex centralization, method allowlist automation, frame size limits, debugger permission documentation, and schema validation timeout.

userStories.title: Validate WebSocket URL path in offscreen document
userStories.description: As a security engineer, I want the offscreen document to validate the WebSocket URL path (not just host) so that a compromised /ws-info response cannot redirect the connection to an arbitrary endpoint on the same host.
acceptanceCriteria: isValidWsOrigin() in offscreen/index.ts checks parsed.pathname === '/ws' after host validation
URLs like ws://localhost:9515/evil or ws://localhost:9515/ws/extra are rejected
Warning is logged when path validation fails (similar to existing protocol/host warnings)
All 3 call sites of isValidWsOrigin continue to work (lines ~214, ~349, ~365)
userStories.notes: File: platform/browser-extension/src/offscreen/index.ts lines 69-88. The function currently only checks parsed.protocol and parsed.host. Add a third check: `if (parsed.pathname !== '/ws') { console.warn(...); return false; }`. The server's WebSocket endpoint is always at /ws — this is hardcoded in http-routes.ts. The offscreen document is NOT covered by bun:test (it's a Chrome extension context), so add a comment explaining the validation and why the path must be /ws.

userStories.title: Add sender validation to offscreen chrome.runtime.onMessage
userStories.description: As a security engineer, I want the offscreen document's message listener to validate that messages come from our own extension so that content scripts or other extensions cannot send ws:send messages to the MCP server.
acceptanceCriteria: chrome.runtime.onMessage listener checks sender.id === chrome.runtime.id at the top of the handler
Messages from other senders are rejected with sendResponse({ sent: false, reason: 'unauthorized sender' }) or silently ignored
A comment explains this is defense-in-depth (Chrome already isolates extension message passing)
ws:send, ws:getState, and ws:setUrl message types are all protected by the check
userStories.notes: File: platform/browser-extension/src/offscreen/index.ts line 322. The listener currently uses `_sender` (unused). Change to `sender` and add at the top of the listener body: `if (sender.id !== chrome.runtime.id) { console.warn('[opentabs:offscreen] Rejected message from unauthorized sender:', sender.id); return false; }`. The `sender` parameter has type `chrome.runtime.MessageSender` which has an `id?: string` field. Use optional chaining for safety: `sender.id !== chrome.runtime.id`. Return `true` at the end to indicate async response if needed — check existing return value pattern.

userStories.title: Centralize plugin name validation regex in browser extension
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the plugin name validation regex to be defined in one place within the browser extension so that inconsistencies between files cannot create validation bypass vulnerabilities.
acceptanceCriteria: A single VALID_PLUGIN_NAME regex is exported from a shared location within the browser extension (e.g., src/constants.ts or src/validation.ts)
iife-injection.ts imports it instead of defining its own SAFE_PLUGIN_NAME
message-router.ts imports it instead of using inline regex at lines 101 and 507
side-panel/App.tsx imports it instead of defining its own VALID_PLUGIN_NAME
No inline plugin name regex patterns remain in any browser extension source file
The isSafePluginName function in iife-injection.ts continues to also check RESERVED_NAMES
userStories.notes: Current duplication: (1) iife-injection.ts:5 defines SAFE_PLUGIN_NAME = /^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$/; (2) message-router.ts:101 uses inline !/^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$/.test(obj.name); (3) message-router.ts:507 uses same inline regex; (4) side-panel/App.tsx:14 defines VALID_PLUGIN_NAME = /^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$/. Create platform/browser-extension/src/constants.ts with `export const VALID_PLUGIN_NAME = /^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$/;` and `export const isValidPluginName = (name: string): boolean => VALID_PLUGIN_NAME.test(name);`. Import in all 4 files. The extension can't use @opentabs-dev/shared at runtime (browser context), so the constant must live within the extension. Also export the RESERVED_NAMES set from constants.ts if iife-injection.ts needs it alongside the regex.

userStories.title: Add test to verify ALLOWED_METHODS covers all methodHandlers
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a test that catches when a new method handler is added to message-router.ts but ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts is not updated, preventing silent 30-second timeouts from dropped messages.
acceptanceCriteria: A shared constants file (e.g., src/known-methods.ts) exports the complete set of WebSocket method names used by the extension
Both ALLOWED_METHODS in offscreen/index.ts and methodHandlers in message-router.ts derive from or are checked against this shared set
A unit test verifies that every key in methodHandlers is present in ALLOWED_METHODS
A unit test verifies that every entry in ALLOWED_METHODS either exists in methodHandlers or is in a documented exempt list (e.g., 'pong' which is handled directly, not via methodHandlers)
userStories.notes: Approach: Create platform/browser-extension/src/known-methods.ts that exports a const array of all method names. In offscreen/index.ts, import it and build ALLOWED_METHODS = new Set(KNOWN_METHODS). In message-router.ts, the methodHandlers Map keys should be a subset of KNOWN_METHODS. Write a test in a new file (e.g., src/known-methods.test.ts) that imports both KNOWN_METHODS and the methodHandlers keys, then asserts they're in sync. The offscreen ALLOWED_METHODS also includes 'pong' which is handled by the offscreen ws.onmessage handler directly (not routed through message-router), so the test should account for that. Methods in KNOWN_METHODS that are NOT in methodHandlers need to be explicitly listed as 'offscreen-only' or 'direct-handled'.

userStories.title: Set WebSocket maxPayloadLength in Bun.serve config
userStories.description: As a security engineer, I want the MCP server to enforce a WebSocket frame size limit at the transport layer so that oversized messages are dropped before being buffered into memory.
acceptanceCriteria: This matches the existing MAX_MESSAGE_SIZE check in extension-protocol.ts
Messages exceeding 10MB are dropped by Bun before reaching the message handler
A comment explains why 10MB matches the application-level check in extension-protocol.ts
userStories.notes: File: platform/mcp-server/src/index.ts lines 181-191. The websocket config currently only has open/message/close handlers. Add `maxPayloadLength: 10 * 1024 * 1024` to the websocket config object. Bun's WebSocket server supports this option (see Bun.serve docs). This is FROZEN CORE code — the comment block says changes require a full process restart. That's fine for a security fix. The existing MAX_MESSAGE_SIZE in extension-protocol.ts (line 37) is also 10MB — keep them in sync. Consider defining a shared constant, but since index.ts is frozen core and extension-protocol.ts is hot-reloadable, a simple duplicate with a comment is acceptable.

userStories.title: Document debugger permission usage in manifest.json
userStories.description: As a security auditor, I want the manifest.json to document why the debugger permission is required so that future audits don't flag it as unnecessary attack surface.
acceptanceCriteria: manifest.json has a comment (or a separate PERMISSIONS.md reference) documenting that 'debugger' is used by network-capture.ts for chrome.debugger.attach/detach/sendCommand
Since JSON doesn't support comments, add a '_permissions_notes' field in manifest.json OR create a brief note in the CLAUDE.md architecture section about which permissions are used and why
The documentation mentions: debugger is used for Network.enable and Runtime.enable via Chrome DevTools Protocol for network capture functionality
userStories.notes: The debugger permission IS actively used in platform/browser-extension/src/network-capture.ts: chrome.debugger.attach (line 168), chrome.debugger.sendCommand for Network.enable and Runtime.enable (lines 177-178), chrome.debugger.detach (lines 149, 180, 201), and chrome.debugger.onEvent.addListener (line 73). This is legitimate — it's used for the browser.captureNetworkTraffic tool. Since JSON doesn't support comments, the best approach is to add a short note in CLAUDE.md under the Chrome Extension section explaining the permission. Keep it brief — just mention debugger is required for network capture via Chrome DevTools Protocol.

userStories.title: Add timeout guard on Ajv schema validation
userStories.description: As a security engineer, I want tool input validation to have a timeout so that pathological schemas cannot hang the MCP server indefinitely.
acceptanceCriteria: The validate(args) call in mcp-setup.ts is wrapped in a timeout mechanism
If validation takes longer than 5 seconds, the tool call is rejected with an error message containing 'validation timed out'
Normal schemas (which complete in microseconds) are not affected by the timeout
The timeout cleanup is handled properly (no leaked timers)
A unit test verifies that normal validation completes successfully under the timeout
userStories.notes: File: platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-setup.ts around line 309. Currently `const valid = lookup.validate(args);` is synchronous. Ajv's validate() is synchronous so wrapping in Promise.race won't help directly. Instead, use a different approach: since Ajv validation is synchronous and the actual risk is very low (schemas are compiled at discovery time from plugin manifests we've already validated), consider one of: (1) Add a max schema complexity check at compilation time (reject schemas with more than N levels of nesting or M allOf/oneOf branches). (2) Wrap the synchronous call in a try-catch with a comment explaining it's safe because schemas are pre-validated. (3) Use AbortController with a worker thread for true timeout. Option 1 is the most practical — add a maxDepth check when compiling the Ajv validator. The validate() itself is already fast for well-formed schemas. If going with runtime timeout, note that Bun supports `setTimeout` in a Promise.race pattern, but the synchronous validate() would need to be run in a worker. A pragmatic approach: add a maxItems/maxProperties constraint to the compiled schema to prevent combinatorial explosion.
